source_signals.title: Albanese didn’t return with shiploads of diesel. That doesn’t mean his Singapore visit wasn’t a success

The storm has caused widespread damage across the Solomon Islands, particularly in Western, Choiseul and Isabel provinces, where schools, clinics and homes have been damaged. A 37-year-old man from Dunmow, who was arrested on suspicion of being in charge of a dog dangerously out of control and causing injury resulting in death, has been bailed until July while inquiries continue, Essex police said on Sunday. The canine, which was a family pet and believed to be a lurcher crossbreed, was seized and tests are under way to formally establish the dog’s breed, the force added. Assistant chief constable Stuart Hooper said: “Our thoughts remain with all those who knew and loved Jamie-Lea. Her young life has been so tragically cut short. “Our detectives are continuing to work around the clock to establish exactly what happened and specialist officers are continuing to support Jamie-Lea’s family. “This is unimaginable for her loved ones and friends and, as such, I would ask people to respect their grief and privacy at this extremely difficult time. “Our officers remain at the scene and anyone with concerns or information can speak with them there or contact us in the usual way.” A post-mortem examination is due to take place on Sunday, police said. at_a_glance: Trump threatens a US blockade of the Strait of Hormuz as talks with Iran falter, raising risks of escalation and global supply shocks.
Oil and jet-fuel strains deepen: European airports warn of shortages and shippers are told not to pay Iranian levies for safe passage.
BBC analysis shows only limited vessel traffic through Hormuz despite a fragile ceasefire, keeping risk premia elevated.

The UK says it will not join any US-led blockade of Hormuz, underscoring allied divisions over next steps.

lead_story.title: Hormuz at the brink: Trump’s blockade threat collides with failed US–Iran talks and a tightening energy squeeze
lead_story.summary: Donald Trump’s threat to blockade the Strait of Hormuz after US–Iran talks collapsed raises the risk of confrontation without resolving the underlying stalemate. Iran has shown willingness to absorb strikes while leveraging control over a chokepoint that carries a large share of global oil and gas flows. Early signs suggest shipping remains sparse despite a fragile ceasefire, feeding fuel shortages and inflation fears from Europe to Asia. Allied positions are diverging – with the UK distancing itself from any blockade – leaving questions over coalition unity, escalation control and how quickly energy markets can stabilise.
body: Donald Trump’s threat to blockade the Strait of Hormuz after US–Iran talks collapsed has pushed the crisis back to the brink without resolving the underlying impasse. The waterway is the world’s most sensitive energy chokepoint, and any attempt to seal it would elevate the risk of direct confrontation while testing global supply chains already under strain, according to multiple reports from the BBC.

The discussions between Washington and Tehran ended without agreement after marathon sessions, leaving a fragile ceasefire and the same strategic standoff. Analysts now frame the conflict as a test of endurance: Iran’s ability to absorb strikes against Trump’s tolerance for the diplomatic, military and economic costs of escalation.

Early shipping signals are discouraging. BBC Verify analysis indicates only a few vessels have crossed Hormuz since the ceasefire, suggesting operators remain cautious amid uncertain guarantees of safe passage. Industry advisers have also urged tankers not to pay any Iranian levies in return for transit, underscoring the legal and insurance complications around navigating the strait.

Energy repercussions are rippling quickly. Europe’s airports warn of potential jet-fuel shortages if Hormuz stays constrained, US inflation has been pushed higher by pump prices, and UK motorists are being told not to expect rapid relief. In Asia, where many economies are heavily reliant on Gulf oil, higher energy costs are intensifying pressure on households and businesses.

Markets are volatile rather than outright panicked, but the direction of travel is clear: tightness in refined fuels such as jet and diesel could worsen before alternative cargoes arrive. Refiners and traders can reroute flows, yet those shifts take time, storage buffers are finite, and uncertainty over transit terms through the Gulf is keeping freight and insurance costs elevated.

Allied positions are starting to diverge. The UK has urged the US and Iran to avoid escalation and has not endorsed a blockade, reflecting European preferences for keeping sea lanes open without provoking a direct clash. That leaves Washington balancing deterrence with coalition management at a moment when unity is central to both maritime security and market confidence.

A formal blockade would be contested under international law and require sustained enforcement in one of the world’s tightest and most surveilled waterways. Iran retains asymmetric options that raise the risk of incidents and miscalculation, from harassment of commercial traffic to the use of proxies, and it has shown a willingness to absorb strikes rather than concede under pressure.

Tehran’s leverage lies in the strait itself: even sparse traffic and intermittent uncertainty can achieve outsized economic effects. The ambiguity over rules of passage, including reports of mooted levies and evolving guidance to shippers, allows Iran to exert pressure without an outright closure, while complicating any US-led effort to reassure markets swiftly.

What happens next will turn on several watchpoints: whether vessel traffic through Hormuz normalises, how quickly insurers reinstate cover at reasonable rates, and if a coordinated maritime security effort emerges with broad allied backing. Energy producers’ decisions, including any output adjustments, and the responses of major importers in Asia, will also shape price dynamics.

Best-case scenarios involve a de facto transit arrangement that restores flows and cools prices; worst-case paths run through attempts to enforce or resist a blockade that triggers direct confrontation and a sharper inflation shock. For now, the energy squeeze is tightening, coalition cohesion is under strain, and despite the saber-rattling, another diplomatic track remains the only credible route to durable stability.

section_features.standfirst: Talks between the US and Iran have faltered without reopening the Strait of Hormuz, even as President Trump’s blockade threat raises the stakes. With a vital artery of energy trade contested, fuel shortages, protests and political pressure are spreading far beyond the Gulf — from Ireland to Australia — while pathways to de-escalation grow narrower.
